entity: Martin Gatton College of Agriculture, Food, and Environment
summary: I'm an assistant professor in the Department of Plant Pathology at the Martin Gatton College of Agriculture, Food, and Environment. I've been here in Kentucky for about eight years now, starting as a postdoc and moving up to assistant professor. My work here involves managing undergraduate programs and doing science outreach, especially in agricultural and medical biotechnology. This college is where I found my academic home after following my wife to Kentucky.

I was born and raised in Phoenix, Arizona, where I spent my youth before heading off to college. Growing up there, I developed interests like scuba diving, which later influenced my early career choices. Phoenix is where my story begins, even though my professional life took me far from there.

I went to college in New Orleans, Louisiana, where I majored in English and Religious Studies, which was quite different from what I do now. During that time, I also got really involved in scuba diving and even became a scuba instructor. New Orleans was a formative place for me academically and personally before I shifted towards biology and plant pathology.

While I was in college, I earned my boat captain's license from the United States Coast Guard. This certification helped me work in the scuba industry, managing dive charters and working in commercial diving. The Coast Guard played a key role in my early career path before I transitioned into science.

My wife got into grad school at the University of Arkansas, which was a turning point for me. I started there as a non-traditional biology student after initially working in the scuba industry. I began washing dishes in a plant pathology lab through a work-study position and eventually pursued my master's and PhD there, shifting my career towards plant pathology and fungal molecular genetics.

entity: VA hospital in Charleston, South Carolina
summary: After finishing my PhD, I did a postdoc at the VA hospital in Charleston, South Carolina, affiliated with the Medical University of South Carolina. There, I conducted human genetics research focused on identifying mutations linked to suicidality, PTSD, and depression in combat veterans. This work was part of a government effort to pre-screen military recruits for genetic predispositions to frontline battle issues.

My postdoctoral research was done at the Medical University of South Carolina in Charleston, where I worked closely with the VA hospital. I transitioned from fungal molecular genetics to human genetics there, studying genetic mutations related to mental health conditions in veterans. This experience broadened my scientific expertise and connected me to human health research.

Much of my research in plant pathology is funded by the United States Department of Agriculture. Being part of the agricultural sciences means our work focuses on agriculturally relevant organisms, and USDA support is crucial for that. It distinguishes our research from other biology fields that might focus on model organisms like mice or salamanders.

In my science outreach work, I often use everyday items from stores like Kroger to help students conduct experiments. For example, we use potatoes bought at Kroger to demonstrate bacterial soft rot and other plant diseases. This makes science accessible and relatable for middle and high school students by using familiar materials.

I've been living in Kentucky for about eight years now, following my wife who got into grad school here. I started as a postdoc, then was a lecturer for five years, and now I'm an assistant professor. Kentucky is where I established my academic career in plant pathology and where I do much of my teaching and community outreach.
